segment-text: PINK PAGES 1 NEW YORK RESTAURANT - EVENING In a fashionable uptown Asian-Fusion eatery, a birthday

celebration takes place. At the head of the table is ALICE HOWLAND, a woman who could be said to have it all: a highflying academic career, a successful marriage, and three healthy grown children. She is whip smart, charming, undeniably beautiful. To her right is her husband, JOHN HOWLAND, also an academic, a bear of a man with a gleam of high intelligence. To her left is her oldest daughter ANNA HOWLAND JONES, a successful lawyer, and handsome husband CHARLIE. One chair at the table is conspicuously empty. ANNA (PRE-LAP)
Anna hands Alice a gift-wrapped bag.
Is that for me?
Exactly, I don't believe it. You don't even look forty, much less
Oh Charlie, please - but thank you. I appreciate it. Can I open this
now?
Yes, open it. Please.
Alice opens the present and gasps. Oh!
Oh I love it.
Oh good.
It's so beautiful.
You can exchange it if it doesn't fit.
No, no, no. I'm not going to. I absolutely love it. You always get me things I love.
PINK PAGES 2
A smart young man bearing a resemblance to John rushes up. This is TOM HOWLAND.
Sorry I'm late..!
Tom kisses his mom on the cheek.
Tom finds a seat.
Isn't...um...Lisa coming?
No, we split up.
Yes, I did notice that your status popped back to single...yet again.
He gives his sister a tight-lipped smile.
I forgot your present. my locker.
Then, to Alice:
I just hope you didn't spend too much.
Don't worry, he didn't.
Where have you been?
The ER was like a madhouse. This one guy came in - six stab wounds. I swear one missed his heart by, seriously, an inch.
No. Tom, it's dinner time. Please.
Not yet it isn't. Nearly.
So what kind of antibiotic do you use..?
As John and Tom engage enthusiastically in a rehash of the poor guy's trauma, Alice turns to Anna and Charlie.
Mom, have you spoken to Lydia?
Yes. She wanted to be here tonight, but she had a really important audition. What for?
A guest spot on some TV thing. Something Enemy.
Anna shakes her head.
Maybe this will be her big break.
Yeah maybe.
Don't hold your breath. Meow.
(laughing) Stop it.
You two must have been something growing up. Why do I picture dolls with heads cut off?
No, my sister and I were very close, actually.
Charlie and Anna both look at her, a little surprised.
Oh...sorry...I was talking about Anna and Lydia.
Oh my goodness, I don't know why I said that.
Anna steps in to change the mood.
Well we're here to celebrate you, Mom. So Dad, what about a toast.
She turns to her dad, who is still rattling on about E.R.
Dad, a toast?!
Oh yes, yes, yes.
They raise their glasses.
To the most beautiful and the most intelligent woman I have known my entire life.
UCLA UNIVERSITY CAMPUS - CORRIDOR - LOS ANGLES - DAY
We follow Alice from behind as she walks down the bright, broad corridor of the academic institution.
My name is Frederick Johnson, I'm an associate professor of cognitive science here at UCLA and I'm here to welcome today's speaker who has just flown in from New York.
FUNCTION ROOM - AN HOUR LATER
A lectern has been set up in front of a projection screen. About FORTY ACADEMICS are sitting, listening. Young, confident, PROFESSOR JOHNSON is introducing Alice as she waits at the side.
PINK PAGES 5
Now in my dissertation I spent about a chapter and a half fairly vituperatively citing today's guest and saying why I thought she was wrong...
The audience laughs, Alice as well.
For the record, every time Alice and I have argued, she's right. Alice Howland is the Lillian Young

Columbia University. She famously wrote her seminal textbook, From Neurons to Nouns, while raising three children - I'm sure getting more than a few "Ah-ha" moments from them - and it is now considered one of the cornerstones of linguistics education all over the world. Please welcome...Dr. Alice Howland. Warm applause from the assembled. behind the lectern.
Alice takes her place
Thank you so much.
She taps a computer sitting on the lectern. A photo of an adorable, bright-eyed baby comes up on the screen behind her. She launches into her talk with great self-assurance.
Most children speak and understand
She taps her computer - a photo of Charles Darwin comes up.
Much has been learned since then but today I'm going to show you some recent studies from my lab in children between the ages of eighteen months and two and a half
She taps again - a picture of a toddler in a kid-friendly

Now, this might sound like it falls into the great academic tradition of knowing more and more about less and less until we know everything about nothing. (audience chuckles) But I hope to convince you that by taking these first baby steps into the...
The smooth, assured flow of Alice's lecture is broken - a word isn't forthcoming.
Into the...
The audience waits patiently.
(deadpanning) I knew I shouldn't have had that champagne. An appreciative murmur. the word.
Alice takes a slight detour round

...into the word stock of a given language... (hits her stride again) ...we will learn crucial information about the interaction between memory and computation that is the very essence of communication.
BLACK CAR -- MOVING -- LOS ANGELES
Alice stares out the window - something is troubling her. She closes her eyes as if to squeeze out a thought.
(under her breath) Lexicon!
Satisfied, she looks at a WORDS WITH FRIENDS game on her iPhone. Moving three letters, she forms the word "HADJ" on a triple.
EXT. APARTMENT BUILDING - KOREATOWN - LATE AFTERNOON
The black car arrives at a two-story run-down Seventies
apartment building. EXT. SECOND FLOOR CORRIDOR

Alice exits the elevator and walks down the corridor of the low-rent building. She passes a room where she glimpses a few young people hanging out listening to indie rock. A little further down, she finds Lydia's door. The door is answered by LYDIA HOWLAND, a feisty young woman with dark hair.
Hi.
ALICE
Hey, sorry. You just caught me by surprise. Come on in. It's messy but I was in the process of cleaning.
LYDIA'S APARTMENT
It's fine, honey. Don't worry.

Alice looks around. A living room adjoins a kitchen, separated only by a counter that is cluttered with left-overs from lunch. There are some pieces of eighties thrift store furniture, some carelessly discarded men's boots.
(picking up a few things) The boys are total slobs. The kitchen is the main battleground, but I've got them in training. So we'll see how that goes.
Alice glances at some photobooths on the wall - Lydia and two young men goofing for the camera.
So where are they?
At work. Doug's at Starbucks. Malcolm does some catering.
I guess that's what they mean by a working actor?
Lydia gives a tight-lipped smile.
Acting is always a sore
L.A. RESTAURANT - NIGHT
The restaurant is typical Silverlake -- hipster Mexican. Lydia has finished eating. Alice is still working on it. ALICE (PRE-LAP)
Doug's gay. And Malcolm and I did have a thing, but it's over. He got me in with Open Space though. It's like the best theater company.
Yes, I heard.
Alice's plate is taken away prematurely by a BUSBOY.
(joking) Thank you. Yeah, I'm done.
It would have been awesome if you saw 'No Exit.'
Daddy said you were really good.
I was all right. Not my best night, but at least he got a sense of it all. Figure out why he's doing - you know - me a solid with the company.
Alice didn't know this. Lydia immediately realizes she shouldn't have said anything.
What do you mean?
It's just really nice of him to help out.
How is he helping out with the company?
It's our responsibility as members to raise equity for our productions we put on. And then eventually we get a cut of the box office - it's like we're shareholders...
So you have to...pay to act, basically?
No. It's a theater group. It's just the reality of the situation. That's how it is here. It's Los Angeles.
Lydia, don't you think it's time you reconsidered things? You're so smart. There's so much more you could be doing with your life...
(interrupting, annoyed) Like going to college?
Yes! Like college.
Yeah...Like we've never talked about that before...every single day of my life. I figured out what I wanted to do and I'm doing it. It's a good thing.
But on whose dime?
You're helping Tom with Med school. You helped Anna with law.
Sweetheart...those are real careers. I just don't want you to limit your choices.
You want to make my choices.
No, I don't.
I'm really happy.
I'm sorry. I don't - I don't want to argue about this. Just forget I said anything.
It's forgotten.
A noise dings on Alice's phone.
You going to check your phone?
Alice looks at the phone.
It's just a game. Your sister and I have developed an obsession with Words with Friends.
(feeling a bit left out) That's cute.
(reading this) Believe me, you don't want to play her. She's a demon.
I'm not surprised.
Alice sighs and looks for a way to re-start the conversation. EXT.
TOWN CAR - MOVING - AFTERNOON
Alice is travelling, once again, now through the streets of New York.
ALICE'S HOME - ENTRY WAY
She enters with her bags and closes the door behind her. John? Hi.
KITCHEN
Depositing her keys in a goldfish bowl, she glances at a small memory board on the wall. No note has been left for her. John?
She gets her iPhone out of her bag and texts John: Where R U?
UPTOWN STREETS - MOMENTS LATER
Now in athletic wear, Alice runs down the cold street. navigates around PEDESTRIANS and MOMS WITH STROLLERS. EXT. She
RIVERSIDE PARK
In the light of the late afternoon, she runs through the trees on a familiar path. EXT.
COLUMBIA CAMPUS -- DAY

Students and academics are going about their business. A young STREET VENDOR is passing out leaflets calling, "Sale at the Shoe Warehouse." Alice runs into the center of the square then slows down. Suddenly, she's not sure of where she is. TIGHT CLOSE-UP OF ALICE THE DEPTH OF FIELD has become very shallow, completely isolating her from her surroundings. The normal University soundscape fades away disconcertingly. She looks from one building to another - from one person to another. Things swim momentarily into focus but there's no context - no bigger picture. Her breathing starts to quicken. She ventures one way a few paces, up some stairs. Her breathing is rapid, her face flushed with fear. She puts her head down, forcing herself to focus.
PINK PAGES 12
Then, she recognizes the Columbia Library Building in front of her. Normality returns. People walk by her, chatting. Everything is in focus now. She heads towards her home. Want one?
STREET VENDOR
ALICE
ALICE'S HOME - ENTRYWAY
There's the sound of a key in the door. JOHN (O.S.)
From the kitchen area, John appears, hair messy, a day's stubble visible. Alice enters, trying not to show the turmoil she's feeling.
I was wondering where you were.
He goes to hug her.
I'm all sweaty.
How did it go? What?
Alice heads to the kitchen.
INT. KITCHEN
UCLA..?
Good. They were really...receptive.
I'm sure they were. Lydia?
Good, y'know... And how was
You guys argue?
Well we spent the whole evening trying not to. You know, I have to say the news that you were bankrolling her theatre company didn't exactly help.
I told you about that.
You didn't.
I thought I did.
You didn't.
Well...you're not gonna like this either, but I still have a lot of things to do at the lab. So maybe you could walk me over there right now...
(annoyed) No. I don't want to go to campus. I just went for a run. I really need to be home now.
(a flicker of concern) Are you okay?
Yeah, I'm fine. I just - you said we were going to spend the evening in.
I did - and we will. The sooner I go, the sooner I'll be back. And we can watch a movie.
Alice takes a breath.
I'm just exhausted.
He kisses her on the forehead and heads off.
Get some rest. Try to relax.
John walks out.
DR. BENJAMIN'S OFFICE - DAY
Alice sits in a chair being questioned by DR. BENJAMIN, an unseen Neurologist. In one long take, we see her trademark confidence start to erode.

I've started forgetting things. Little annoying things likes words and names. And I got lost completely lost - running on campus. I talked to my doctor about it and she said it could be menopause and the last time I had a period was in February but then I had one in September right before my birthday so...
Are you taking any pills, any medication, supplements?
I take a multivitamin, flax seed oil, calcium, iron and occasionally a sleeping pill when I travel.
Have you had any head injuries? No.
Would you consider yourself depressed, or under undue stress at the moment? No. it.
I work a lot but I thrive on
How's your sleeping? It's fine. night.
Like seven hours a
Now I'm going to ask you to remember a name and address. I'll ask you for it again later, okay? (she nods) John Black, 42 Washington Street, Hoboken. Can you repeat that for me?
John Black, 42 Washington Street, Hoboken. Good.
How old are you?
What's today's date? ALICE
Where are we?
We are on the third floor of New York Presbyterian.
Can you spell 'water' for me? W-A-T-E-R.
Now spell it for me backwards. R-E-T-A-W.
Can you tell me what you see on these cards?
Pliers. A ladder. A sea horse. screwdriver. An elephant.
It seems absurd for Alice to be answering such childish questions.
Tell me about your parents.
They're both dead. My mother and sister died in a car accident when I was eighteen. My father died in 1999 of liver failure. Hepatitis? Cirrhosis.
He was an alcoholic.
And what was he like towards the end? It's an uncomfortable subject for Alice.
Incoherent. Incontinent. To tell you the truth we didn't see a lot of each other. He lived in New Hampshire and...we weren't that close.
Sorry. Do you have any other siblings? No.
I only had the one.
Now can you tell me the name and address I asked you to remember? A wall.
Alice tries to recall.
Um...John Black.
And the address?
She takes a moment.
Damn it, I forgot. DR. BENJAMIN
42 Argyle street, Cole Street,
A pause. She shakes her head.
I was distracted, talking about my parents. Can we do that one again?
There's no need to at this time. Okay.
I'd like to do an MRI, just to rule some things out.
Like what? Because I think I have a brain tumor.
It's just a precaution. But it will also allow us to see if there are any lesions or signs of a stroke. Find out what's going on in there.
Alice nods, absorbing this.
Okay - what should I do in the meantime?
You can get plenty of exercise. It's always good to get the blood pumping. And drink lots of water, hydration is excellent for the memory.
Alice takes this in.
And the next time that you come and see me, can you bring in someone that knows you well? Your husband or a close relative?
After a brief hesitation, Alice nods.
ALICE'S NEIGBORHOOD -- DAY
Snow has fallen. The busy streets of New York are suddenly, and magically, quiet. INT.
ALICE'S HOME - KITCHEN - AFTERNOON

We see winter squash chopped; brussel sprouts peeled, a turkey in the oven being checked. Alice is busily preparing Christmas dinner. She goes over to the far wall murmuring... ALICE Stethoscope. Millennium. A timer rings. Hedgehog. Lifting a dish cloth on a memory board, she checks three words - Stethoscope. Millennium. Hedgehog. Erasing the words, she thinks a moment and writes, "Cathode, Pomegranate, Trellis." She places the cloth back over the words and resets the timer. MOMENTS LATER Alice is peeling carrots. There's the sound of someone coming in the front door.
Hi.
Come on in.
Tom appears with a new GIRLFRIEND. Hey Mom.
Good to see you.
This is Jenny. I'm Alice. join us.
I'm so happy you could
Thank you for having me.
Of course. Sweetheart, why don't you guys go in the and get a drink and grab the - cheese thing. All right? I'm so happy you could join, I really am, Jenny. Thank you.
Tom and Jenny head to the living room.
INT. LIVING ROOM
It smells so good in here. Beautiful.
Tom admires the table spread.
KITCHEN -- MOMENTS LATER
Alice assembles milk, sugar, white chocolate, challah bread on the counter. She opens a box of eggs and stares blankly at them.
(to herself) How many goddam eggs..?
The timer goes off again.
Cathode. Pomegranate...Trellis.
She goes over to the memory board, checks the results. again! She erases the words as Lydia appears.
Hey!!
They hug warmly.
Merry Christmas.
Aw, Merry Christmas.
How was your flight?
Actually awful. This guy coughed all over me the entire time.
I hope you don't get sick.
Lydia glances at the ingredients on the counter.
Does this mean what I think it means? Yes.
Bread pudding!
It's your favorite.
ANNA (O.S.)
Anna and Charlie enter the kitchen in expensive casual-wear contrasting with Lydia's thrift-store chic. Anna hugs her mother.
Have you seen my latest Words With Friends?
Oh no. I haven't had time yet, sweetheart.
It's a real zinger.
PINK PAGES 21
JEALOUSY on a triple.
Don't spoil it.
It's already trending on Twitter.
Lydia greets her sister. Alice is distracted, staring at the bread pudding ingredients, as the sisters attempt some friendly conversation.
How's California?
It's great.
Warmer than here..? Yes.
Definitely warmer than here.
Okay now, all get outta here. I have to concentrate or there'll be no food.
Where's dad?
He's upstairs.
Lydia goes into the living room.
Can I help you with anything?
No, I'm fine. Just go get a drink or something. Okay.
It's going to be delicious.
Oh I hope so.
It will be. I can't wait.
Anna and Tom go into the living room. for a bread pudding recipe. Alice checks her phone
DINING ROOM - AN HOUR LATER
Anna, Charlie, John, Tom and his girlfriend, are seated drinking wine.
It's a very big decision. We're excited. The place was recommended by a couple of friends who'd been trying for years and have just had their first child. So we'll see...
I've heard good things about Wellspring.
Good. It's expensive, but we obviously want it to work.
I can't believe you're going to be a Mom. I know!!
They carry on chatting as Alice enters carrying a soup tureen.
Here we are...
She places the tureen in the center of the table.
What kind of soup is it?
It's butternut squash. Nice!
Alice notices Jenny sitting next to Tom.
Hi, I'm Alice. I'm so happy you could join us.
I'm - Jenny.
Jenny looks a little startled. Alice takes a breath. Lydia glances over at her.
It's really nice to meet you. Well I think I deserve a glass of wine after all that, don't you?
You want white?
Yes. Thank you. have a glass?
Does everyone
Yes, we do!
Let's have a toast.
To Christmas!! They clink glasses.
DR. BENJAMIN'S OFFICE - DAY
Alice is nervously listening as the doctor reviews her file. DR. BENJAMIN (PRE-LAP) No cerebral vascular disease, no evidence of any stroke, no masses.
(under her breath) Thank God.

And your blood work came back completely clear. But what worries me are the memory tests that I sent you for. You have sporadic memory impairment totally out of proportion to your age and there is evidence of decline in your level of mental function. (MORE)
I think we should do a PET scan. It's similar to an MRI but it can pick up things at the molecular level...
(interrupting) I know what a PET scan is but what in particular are you looking for?
I want to see if the results are consistent with Alzheimer's disease. It would be rare for someone as young as yourself. But you do fit the criteria.
Alice nods, absorbing this.
Now last time you came I asked you to bring a close relative.
I really didn't think that would be necessary.
Next time, for sure. Okay..?
KITCHEN - NIGHT
John is at the counter top, reading e-mails on his computer. At the sink, scrubbing a pot, Alice responds to him almost on autopilot.
"...enhancement of endogenous levels of beta endorphin has not so far been shown to reduce the incidence of metastasis in the distant cell sites."
That's not good.
Tell me about it. We've been working on this for months! "The beta-endorphin doesn't significantly impact the microphage
How about the cytokines?
Elevated. Slightly. Oh god back to square one. Are we both here on the seventeenth?
I don't remember.
Phil and Diane were saying they might come to town for the weekend.
No reply. John looks up at her. scrubbing. She is still determinedly
How long are you going to keep at that thing?
Until it's clean...
BEDROOM - NIGHT
John is sleeping soundly. the clock. It's 2.15. Unable to sleep, Alice looks at
BATHROOM - LATER
She enters, switching on the too-bright light, then looks at herself in the mirror, face scrunched. Opening the bathroom cabinet, she takes out some sleeping pills. INT.
BEDROOM - NIGHT
It's now 4:35 a.m. Alice remains awake. Her eyes open, her thoughts racing. Deliberately, she turns on the bedside lamp and touches John's shoulder.
John...John, sweetheart.
Wake up. He groans, shifts around under the covers. his eyes. He groggily opens
Hmmm... What time is it?
I need to talk to you...I've...I've got something wrong with me.
PINK PAGES 26
He gradually starts to wake up.
What are you talking about..?
I've been seeing a neurologist.
You've been seeing a neurologist? Why?
They think it might be early onset Alzheimer's.
What? That, that makes absolutely no sense.
I didn't want to tell you because they don't know anything for sure but I've been doing all these tests and I'm really scared.
John sits up in bed.
Ali, that is completely insane.
I got lost when I was running on campus awhile ago. I can't remember appointments. Words...
Honey, we all have memory lapses. That's a sign of getting older. The other day I forgot the word 'glucose.'
It's not like that. It's like
something just drops out under me.
But there's no diagnosis yet? No.
Well I think that this is ridiculous. It's complete bullshit, you don't have Alzheimer's.
He reaches out again for her, but she pulls away. Her emotion, stoked by a sleepless night and a failed Ambien, turns to anger.
God dammit! Why won't you take me seriously? I know what I'm feeling. And it feels like my brain is fucking dying. And everything I've worked for in my entire life is going. It's all going...
Come here don't cry.
In his eyes we see care and concern but also the first signs
KITCHEN - MORNING
Alice sits at the counter in a baggy sweatshirt. John is dutifully preparing some cereal. They both look exhausted.
What time is your tutorial?
Well, that gives you some time. I'm going to be a little late because I have a departmental meeting. But when I get back maybe we can do dinner, have some Thai food?
Yeah. Okay. I'm okay...last night just got the better of me, that's all.
Their routine conversation seems almost stripped of meaning, yet they do what people do - they carry on.
Well two things; I think it's way too early to jump to any conclusions and whatever happens, I'm here.
She looks at him, weighing him, wondering about the future.
DOCTOR BENJAMIN'S OFFICE
A computer shows a brightly colored cross-section of a brain. Alice's brain.
You can clearly see in here. The red areas are high in beta amyloid. The build up has probably been ongoing for several years. I'm sorry.
John sits beside Alice. He is not buying it.
There is evidence of high amyloid in older people with normal cognitive functioning, correct?
Yes but not in someone as young as Alice. At least, it would be extremely rare.
I also read that high amyloid is associated with conditions other than Alzheimer's.
Unfortunately, in Alice's case this corroborates the clinical symptoms
she's presenting.
Shouldn't any diagnosis be accompanied by a genetic test?
John, hold on a minute, please.
Actually, I was going to suggest that. (MORE)

In a case like this - with the onset being so early - we'd like to check for presenilin mutations. That would be an indicator of Familial Alzheimer's Disease which is a rarer form. We can make an appointment for you to see a genetic counsellor. This news really hits Alice. effort to control her voice.
When she speaks, it takes an
So this concerns my children too? Yes.
I assume if I have the gene, the chances of passing it on to each one is fifty-fifty?
I'm afraid so.
And if they are carrying the gene, what are the odds of them developing the disease?
I'm afraid it's 100%.
She nods, still trying to stay calm as her eyes betray a rush of emotion.
INT. MEDICAL BUILDING ELEVATORS -- DAY
Alice and John wait by the elevators. There's a tremendous weight upon them. For a moment, neither can speak.
The kids will be here for our anniversary.
I don't think we should tell them. Not until we have the test results.
We will by then. week...
If I go in next PINK PAGES 30
Suddenly, John leans forward and punches the button.
Where the hell is this goddam elevator? Here we are in a major
hospital, and they only have one elevator running.
John, it's okay.
We could have taken the stairs by now.
It doesn't matter.
The elevator arrives and they get in silently.
LIVING ROOM - DAY
Anna, Charlie and Tom are on the couch, Lydia sits apart. Alice wants to make an announcement but the words just won't come.
Well, um everybody...
What's going on? Oh boy.
She looks at John.
Are you guys breaking up, or...
No. It's nothing like that.
Mom are you sick..?
I've been seeing a neurologist for the past few months and I have Alzheimer's disease. Early onset.
There is a sense of unease and shock in the room.
That...That makes no sense. Are you sure? It may be...
There's no doubt. She has the disease.
But at her age...
It's rare but it's been confirmed.
(tearing up) You're so young Mom. I don't understand that.
I had noticed one or two things. You didn't know Tom's girlfriend when she came over at Christmas-ANNA (under her breath) Lydia!
Anna shakes her head at Lydia.
What medications are you on?
Right now Aricept and Numenda.
And they can slow its progress? No.
(as Alice tries to talk) I'm afraid not. They can help alleviate the symptoms but not actually prevent the disease.
John! The thing is that the type of Alzheimers I have is very rare. And, well, it's familial -- it's passed on genetically.
We believe she got it from her father. And of course we're very worried about the three of you. Now there is a test you can take. But it's completely up to you whether you want to find out or not.
Anna starts to cry.
I'm sorry.
Lydia sits on her own, quietly
I'm sorry...
BATHROOM - DAY

Alice reaches for a pill container with different capsules for each day of the week. She empties out Wednesday, starts to down them with water. John passes by the doorway. regimen. He doesn't like to watch her
COLUMBIA LECTURE HALL - DAY

TWO DOZEN STUDENTS are waiting. Alice enters, flustered, opens the computer and clicks on the folder that says "Linguistics Classes." It contains six files: Syntax, Acquisition, Semantics, Comprehension, Phonology, and Pathologies. Looking at the list, she is momentarily confused - which lecture is she giving? Meanwhile, her students are starting to get restless.
Can anyone tell me what it says on the syllabus for today?
A few hands shoot up and a voice calls out "Phonology."
And can anyone tell me what phonology is..? No answers.
She clicks on the appropriate file.
"Phono" is from the Greek word "phone" meaning sound, and phonology, broadly speaking, is the study of the sounds of language. It should be carefully distinguished from phonetics... EXT.
COLUMBIA CAMPUS -- DAY
Bundled up against the cold, Alice walks briskly across the campus. Her phone goes off. It's Anna. Hi Anna.
ANNA (O.S.) ALICE Are you okay?
I got the results, I'm positive.
Oh God, Anna. I'm sorry. so sorry.
I'm so
Tom turned out negative and Lydia didn't want to know. But I'm positive.
Baby, how you feeling?
I'm okay. One good thing, at least I found out now before the next IUI session. I guess there's a way they can test the embryos. So we can be sure - that the baby'll be okay.
Anna, where are you? I've got a lecture but I can cancel it and I can be right there.
No no, Mom. It's okay. Charlie.
No, I want to. I want to be there. I can come.
No, please don't worry. fine.
I love you, sweetheart.
I love you. Bye. Bye.
She walks off across the campus alone. INT.
ALICE'S HOME - KITHCEN -- DAY
Alice reaches into the fridge and stops for a moment. There's a bottle of shampoo on the top shelf. She picks it up and looks at it.
LIBRARY BUILDING -- DAY
Back at the university, Alice walks round the rotunda.
COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY - HEAD OF DEPARTMENT'S OFFICE - DAY
The Head of Department, ERIC WELLMAN, is an avuncular fifty year-old with an intimating air. He reads from his computer.
"I had been looking forward to this course my sophomore year but I was thoroughly disappointed. The content was often muddled and delivered with little focus or care."
Alice listens uncomfortably.
"Class was a waste of time. I ended up just following the on-line version." (then the next) "I found Linguistics 201 very erratic. (MORE)
